question: What are the three types of tests in Flutter and when to use each?
answer:
Testing pyramid (more → fewer as you go up):

Unit Tests — fastest, most numerous:
• Single function, class, or method in isolation
• Mock all dependencies
• Run with: flutter test test/unit/

Widget Tests — medium speed, medium coverage:
• Single widget or small widget tree
• testWidgets() + WidgetTester
• Can simulate user interactions (tap, scroll, type)
• Run with: flutter test test/widget/

Integration Tests — slowest, fewest, highest confidence:
• Full app flows on real device or emulator
• Uses integration_test package or patrol
• Covers E2E user journeys

Also: Golden Tests — screenshot comparison for catching UI regressions.

question: How do you write golden tests in Flutter?
answer:
Golden tests (snapshot tests) compare a widget's rendered output pixel-by-pixel against a saved "golden" reference image. They catch unintended visual regressions.

Workflow:
1. Write test with matchesGoldenFile()
2. Run flutter test --update-goldens to generate reference images
3. Commit the golden files to source control
4. CI runs tests — fails if pixels differ

Gotchas:
• Golden images are platform-dependent (macOS vs Linux vs Windows render slightly differently)
• Run goldens on a fixed platform (usually Linux in CI)
• Use goldenFileComparator to set tolerance for minor anti-aliasing differences

question: How do you test widgets that depend on InheritedWidgets (Theme, MediaQuery, etc.)?
answer:
When testing widgets that depend on InheritedWidgets:

1. Wrap in MaterialApp or WidgetsApp for Theme/MediaQuery
2. Use MediaQuery.withClampedTextScaling() for MediaQuery
3. Provide mocked InheritedWidgets
4. Use testWidgets and pumpWidget

Common inherited dependencies:
• Theme
• MediaQuery (size, orientation, padding)
• Navigator
• Localizations
• Provider

question: How do you choose between mocktail and mockito for mocking dependencies?
answer:
Both let you fake classes for tests; the choice mostly comes down to ergonomics and null safety.

mocktail (modern, recommended):
• No code generation — just `class MockX extends Mock implements X {}`
• Works great with sealed classes, records, and Dart 3 features
• Stubbing is closure-based: `when(() => repo.fetch()).thenAnswer((_) async => [...])`
• Forces you to register fallback values for non-nullable arg matchers (`registerFallbackValue(...)`)
• Smaller API surface, less boilerplate

mockito (older, build_runner-based):
• `@GenerateMocks([X])` annotation, `dart run build_runner build` produces `MockX`
• Generated `mocks.mocks.dart` files clutter the test/ directory
• Requires casts and `as Mock` plumbing in places
• Still useful for legacy projects already invested in it

Guidelines:
• New projects → mocktail. Less ceremony, no codegen.
• Mocking concrete classes (not interfaces): mocktail handles it (`class MockDio extends Mock implements Dio {}`); mockito needs `@GenerateNiceMocks([MockSpec<Dio>()])`.
• For TIME and external systems prefer fakes (hand-written stubs implementing the same interface). Mocks should verify CALL CONTRACTS; fakes provide BEHAVIOR.

Common pitfalls regardless of library:
• Stubbing arg matchers without registerFallbackValue → cryptic errors at test run
• Re-stubbing on the same method without `reset()` — old stubs linger
• Mocking too deeply — mocking a Dio Response is brittle; prefer a fake API client

question: What's the difference between pump, pumpAndSettle, pumpFor, and runAsync in widget tests?
answer:
Widget tests run inside a fake clock — `Future.delayed(Duration(seconds: 5))` doesn't wait 5 seconds, it just schedules a timer the test can advance. The pump variants control how time advances.

tester.pump([Duration]):
• Runs the next frame. With no argument, advances 0 ms (just rebuilds).
• With a Duration, advances the clock by that duration and runs intervening frames.
• Use to step through animations frame by frame, or to flush a single setState.

tester.pumpAndSettle([Duration interval]):
• Repeatedly pumps until the binding reports no scheduled frames
• Default `interval: 100ms`; default total timeout: 10 minutes (yes, really)
• Use to skip past a finished animation or wait for the tree to be idle
• ❌ Will hang forever on infinite animations (CircularProgressIndicator, repeating Lottie)

tester.pumpAndSettle(Duration timeout):
• Same as above but with explicit cap; if not idle by then, throws

tester.pump(timeout) cycles vs. pumpFrames:
• Use `tester.pumpFrames(target, totalDuration)` for animations of known length

tester.runAsync<T>(action):
• Executes `action` against the REAL clock. Use it for code that must use a real Future (HTTP-like SDKs, timers under the hood).
• Inside `runAsync`, you cannot call pump — you're outside the test scheduler.
• Required when your code uses `dart:io` HttpClient under the hood, or anything that schedules outside the FakeAsync

Gotchas:
• `pumpAndSettle` near a CircularProgressIndicator hangs — if your loading state shows one, replace with an indeterminate finder before settling, or pump for a fixed duration.
• Network mocks should resolve their Futures via the test scheduler (`Future.value(...)`). Real I/O needs runAsync.

question: How do you write integration tests with integration_test or patrol?
answer:
Integration tests run on a real device or emulator and exercise the whole app — UI, business logic, networking (typically faked at the boundary). They catch the bugs unit and widget tests can't.

integration_test (official):
• Same Test API as widget tests, but runs against a real Flutter engine
• Lives in `integration_test/` directory
• `IntegrationTestWidgetsFlutterBinding.ensureInitialized();` in main
• Run: `flutter test integration_test`
• Limited to Flutter UI — cannot interact with native dialogs, system permissions, push notifications, biometric prompts

patrol (community, more powerful):
• Adds NATIVE control: tap system permission dialogs, type into native text fields, use the system back button reliably, scroll Android settings
• Critical for full E2E flows that touch permissions or share/intent flows
• Uses its own test runner (`patrol test`) that wraps integration_test
• Heavier setup; runs slower than vanilla integration_test

When to use which:
• Pure-Flutter flows — integration_test is enough
• Anything involving permissions, push, share, app settings — patrol
• CI cost matters → keep integration_tests fast, use patrol only for the few E2Es that absolutely need it

Fundamentals (both):
• Replace network with a fake (in-process server, recorded fixtures)
• Wipe storage between tests
• Mock time/date to keep results deterministic
• Tag flakies and run them off the critical path

question: How do you mock HTTP/Dio in tests without real network calls?
answer:
Real HTTP is forbidden in CI — flaky, slow, and depends on staging up-time. Three layers of fake at the network boundary, from least to most invasive:

1) Inject a fake repository
• Replace the data source (UserRepository) with a hand-written Fake. The Dio instance never gets called.
• Cleanest separation; widget tests don't even know HTTP exists.
• Use this when you can.

2) Mock Dio's adapter (http_mock_adapter / DioAdapter)
• Replace `dio.httpClientAdapter` with a controlled adapter that matches request paths and returns canned responses
• Lets you assert that the right URL/method/body was hit
• Use when testing the data layer itself (the repository → API code path)

3) Local in-process server
• Run `shelf` on a random port; point the app's base URL at it
• Realistic — exercises real serialization and headers
• Heavier setup; valuable for integration tests where you want as much real code as possible

Which to use when:
• Unit/widget tests of UI/Bloc → fake repository (option 1)
• Repository tests → DioAdapter (option 2)
• Integration tests of full flows → in-process server (option 3)

Gotcha: tests that check `requestOptions.headers` need the SAME interceptor stack as production. Don't strip the auth interceptor in tests — verify it adds the header.

question: How do you measure code coverage and gate it in CI?
answer:
Coverage tells you which lines of source ran during tests. It's a useful indicator and a terrible target — chase covered behaviour, not number-go-up.

Flutter generates `coverage/lcov.info` when you run `flutter test --coverage`. From there:

• Render an HTML report locally with `genhtml` for browsing
• Upload to Codecov / Coveralls — they comment on PRs with diffs
• Gate CI: fail the build if coverage drops below a threshold or the PR adds untested code

What to exclude:
• Generated files (`.g.dart`, `.freezed.dart`, `.gr.dart`) — they don't carry your logic
• `main.dart` boot — usually thin glue with little to test
• Auto-generated GraphQL/protobuf serialization
• Use `// coverage:ignore-line` and `// coverage:ignore-file` annotations sparingly

What to chase:
• Domain logic and reducers (Bloc on<>, Notifier methods) — high value, fast tests
• Error paths — coverage often misses 4xx and 5xx branches; tests there are very valuable
• Pure utility functions — easy to test, easy to break

What to deprioritize:
• 100% widget coverage — golden + integration cover the rendered output more meaningfully
• Trivial getters/setters — no bug surface

CI pattern that works in practice:
• `flutter test --coverage` on PRs
• `dart pub global activate remove_from_coverage` to strip generated files
• Codecov action with a `target: 80%, threshold: 1%` for the diff (a PR can lower coverage by at most 1%)
• Don't fail builds when coverage drops because you wrote a comment — measure the diff, not the absolute
code_example:
# .github/workflows/test.yml
name: Test + Coverage
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: subosito/flutter-action@v2
        with: { flutter-version: '3.22.0' }
      - run: flutter pub get
      - run: flutter test --coverage

      # Strip generated files from the report
      - run: |
          dart pub global activate remove_from_coverage
          dart pub global run remove_from_coverage:remove_from_coverage \
            -f coverage/lcov.info \
            -r '\.g\.dart$' \
            -r '\.freezed\.dart$' \
            -r '\.gr\.dart$' \
            -r 'lib/main\.dart$' \
            -r 'lib/generated/.*'

      - name: Upload to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          files: coverage/lcov.info
          fail_ci_if_error: true

# ── Local report ──
# flutter test --coverage
# genhtml coverage/lcov.info -o coverage/html
# open coverage/html/index.html

# ── codecov.yml — gate the diff, not the absolute ──
# coverage:
#   status:
#     project:
#       default:
#         target: auto                  # don't enforce a fixed % 
#         threshold: 1%                  # allow 1% drop max
#     patch:
#       default:
#         target: 80%                    # new code must be >= 80%
# ignore:
#   - lib/main.dart
#   - lib/generated/**
#   - '**/*.g.dart'
#   - '**/*.freezed.dart'

// ── Inline ignores ──
int expensiveCompute(int n) {
  // coverage:ignore-line — debug helper, not worth testing
  if (kDebugMode) print('compute($n)');
  return n * 2;
}

question: How do you test a screen that uses Riverpod or BLoC?
answer:
Both libraries provide first-class test support; the patterns are very similar — inject mocks/fakes at the dependency boundary, then assert state OR rendered UI.

Riverpod:
• `ProviderContainer` for unit tests — no widget tree needed
• `overrides:` swaps any provider for a fake (e.g. mock the apiClientProvider, the rest of the graph still composes)
• In widget tests, wrap with `ProviderScope(overrides: [...], child: ...)`
• Pure-Dart logic tests are very fast — no `pumpWidget` overhead

BLoC:
• `blocTest` from package:bloc_test for Bloc/Cubit logic — script events, expect states
• In widget tests, use `BlocProvider.value(value: mockBloc, ...)` to inject
• `MockBloc` and `whenListen(stream, initialState: ...)` simulate state sequences without running the real bloc

Shared rules:
• Test logic in unit tests; test rendering in widget tests; don't test logic by inspecting widgets — slow and brittle
• Reset between tests — fresh ProviderContainer / fresh Bloc / clean storage
• Don't reach into private state. Test the observable behavior (state stream, rendered UI).
• Cancel subscriptions and dispose containers in tearDown to avoid leaks across tests

Both ecosystems play well with mocktail for the dependency layer; use bloc_test specifically for the bloc-flow assertions.
